# you asked

message time: 2026-03-30 19:10:47

Here are the corrections from my mentor: 
–
–
–
For Abraham:
“Within the LHC, the Compact Muon Solenoid (CMS) experiment captures the
paths of charged particles through a powerful magnetic field, aiming to
distinguish accurate tracks amidst the complex particle interactions”
This is a little misleading. The magnetic field serves only to curve the
paths of charged particles, so we are able to infer the charge and
momentum of a charged particle _if_ we are able to reconstruct its track.
“…reconstructing particle trajectories through linear segments”
See my comment for Alejandro’s LST statement. I want to highlight that it
builds tracks piece-by-piece such that it can be massively parallelized.
“However, due to its inherent sequential nature, LST has limitations in handling
increasingly intricate scenarios…”
LST is actually _not_ inherently sequential. In fact, it is inherently
massively parallelizable. The algorithm we use _currently_ (which is _not_
LST) is inherently sequential. LST was designed to address this for the
HL-LHC where a sequential algorithm will no longer be usable.
“…highlighting the imperative of harnessing the power of Machine Learning
(ML) techniques”
Actually, it is not clear that ML techniques could ever be faster than LST,
since LST is so parallelizable. ML is compute-intensive, which can be
difficult to use in areas like ours, where we want to run very quickly. ML
techniques instead are interesting for two reasons: (1) they could be
better at reconstructing tracks, (2) there is active research/development
for ML inference platforms that promise very good speeds. If (1) is true,
then we can work on (2) to actually see if we can use these things at the
HL-LHC.
“The convergence of the loss function during training, influenced by the
hidden layer size and model hyperparameters, underscores the symbiotic
relationship between advanced ML and the progressive analysis of particle
tracks”
I think the main result is not mentioned here. Instead of this sentence, I
would add a sentence that says instead what we ultimately found (i.e. that
the DNN performs just as well as the simplest GNN).

Can you redact again the paragraph considering where each observation is?

In the realm of particle physics, the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) stands as a colossal accelerator in Geneva, Switzerland, with its intricate network of superconducting magnets propelling particles to immense energies for experimental collisions. Within the LHC, the Compact Muon Solenoid (CMS) experiment captures the paths of charged particles through a powerful magnetic field, aiming to distinguish accurate tracks amidst the complex particle interactions. Addressing the challenge of efficient track identification, the Line Segment Tracking (LST) algorithm emerges as a solution, reconstructing particle trajectories through linear segments. Notably, LST's modular approach allows for parallelizability, a crucial attribute in tackling the intricate scenarios posed by the forthcoming High-Luminosity LHC (HL-LHC). However, given its inherent sequential nature, LST encounters limitations when faced with increasingly complex scenarios, thereby underscoring the imperative of harnessing the power of Machine Learning (ML) techniques.

This pivotal role of ML is exemplified in our architecture, which leverages Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) with varying hidden layer sizes to process Linear Segments (LS), culminating in an output neuron discerning the authenticity of the track. The convergence of the loss function during training, influenced by the hidden layer size and model hyperparameters, underscores the symbiotic relationship between advanced ML and the progressive analysis of particle tracks. Our investigation unveils the comparable performance of our DNNs, even against the complexity of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), thus illuminating the potential of innovative techniques in the evolving landscape of particle track analysis.

# you asked

message time: 2026-03-30 19:10:47

Keep this info in mind: The LHC is the world’s largest and most powerful particle accelerator. It was built in 2008 at Geneva, Switzerland and it consists of a 27km ring of superconducting magnets with accelerating structures that boost the energy of the particles along the way. These structures consist of around 1,600 magnets with different sizes, shapes and types. We collide these particles at various points to conduct different experiments within the LHC. One of these experiments is the CMS, which is the one we worked with.

One central feature of the CMS is that in its inner layer it contains a huge solenoid magnet that produces a very strong magnetic field. This magnetic field causes charged particles to curve as they move through it and this is what helps us determine the particle's charge and momentum. 

This is the representation of just one track but the challenge comes in when we have many particles and we have to create the particle tracks. So eventually we face this problematic that when tracks increase, time also increases to predict which tracks are right and which ones are wrong to take into account. We propose LST as our algorithm that is going to solve this problem. 

LST refers to Line Segment Tracking which we use to reconstruct the trajectories of the particles that pass through the detector in a linear form which are represented as line segments as we see in the image. Overall what we want is to get all the correct hits and discard all the incorrect ones by having our algorithm tell us if it’s a right track or not. LST is a much faster solution and as accurate to how we have been previously predicting the tracks.

GNN vs DNN

Machine learning is a field that involves various techniques where computers learn patterns and relationships from data. One technique being the neural network. A neural network consists of interconnected nodes that process data and learn to make predictions.

A Deep Neural Network is composed of multiple layers of interconnected nodes. In our case we did 2 hidden layers which is the minimum for it to be considered a DNN and 200 nodes per hidden layer. There are two stages in the DNN which are forward propagation and backward propagation. Forward propagation is the initial step in training where input data goes through the network's layers. The output of one layer becomes the input for the next layer and so on until it reaches the final output. In our second stage we make the adjustment to the weights and biases through the use of a loss function. This function measures the error in prediction accuracy, guiding our network to bring its predicted output closer to the actual target values.

A GNN uses several neural networks to analyze data that can be arranged in a graph which is the main data structure of a GNN. A graph is composed of nodes and edges, where in our case the nodes represent particle tracks and the edges the relationships between particles. At the core of GNN is the concept of message passing. This is where the magic happens. Message passing enables nodes in the graph to communicate with their neighboring nodes, sharing and exchanging valuable information to eventually predict outcomes. 

And so, if we made this comparison to find the same solution, who could be more precise, our DNN that contains a single neural network or a GNN that is built by several neural networks?

Architecture
For this task, we used two DNNs consisting of an input layer of 17 neurons, where 14 of them represent the two nodes that form an LS, and the other 3 represent the line that spans between the two nodes, also known as the edge. The rest comprises two hidden layers of 200 neurons or 32 neurons, depending on how big the model should be, and finally an output layer comprised of just one neuron that computes if the LS is fake or is it not.

One thing to notice is that for the training rounds of the model, the number of neurons inside the aforementioned ‘hidden layer’ influences the convergence of the loss function. This influence is also affected by the hyperparameters used for the model’s training.

“The main goal of the DNN was to employ cutting-edge line segment tracking
techniques to predict the validity of particle tracks identified by the CMS
Detector”
Not exactly. In my words: the Line Segment Tracking (LST) algorithm is
generically designed to reconstruct all particle tracks from a massive point
cloud (really, we are only able to reconstruct some % of them). This is
done in an iterative manner, building tracks piece-by-piece such that the
algorithm can be massively parallelized. We look at one of the earliest
steps: when we are building line segments (LSs), the first real “piece” that
we build in our piece-by-piece algorithm. We train the DNN to identify line
segments that actually lie along a real particle’s trajectory.
Thus, the statement is more correctly something like the last sentence
above. By the way, we chose this task because, in principle, if the DNN (or
any ML algo) is very very good, we could just use it to filter all of the fake
LSs, then connect up all LSs to form our final tracks.
“The DNN was intricately designed to process these curved trajectories,
determining whether a specific track is reliable for further analysis or should
be excluded due to inaccuracies.”
Just a minor comment here: the DNN does not process “tracks” in how we
defined “tracks;” it instead processes what we call line segments, which
can be more generically described as the smallest component of a full
“track,” which we define to be the entire trajectory of a particle.
“Furthermore, we conducted an in-depth analysis using Receiver Operating
Characteristic (ROC) curves to compare the performance of our DNN with a

# gemini response

Thank you for providing this additional information and feedback from your mentor. I'll incorporate these points into the existing text to create a revised summary.

"In the realm of particle physics, the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) stands as a colossal accelerator in Geneva, Switzerland, with its intricate network of superconducting magnets propelling particles to immense energies for experimental collisions. The LHC, constructed in 2008, features a 27km ring of superconducting magnets equipped with accelerating structures that elevate particle energy levels. These structures, comprising around 1,600 magnets of diverse sizes and shapes, facilitate particle collisions at various points within the LHC, powering a multitude of experiments. Among these experiments, the Compact Muon Solenoid (CMS) is distinguished by an inner solenoid magnet generating a robust magnetic field that bends charged particles' trajectories, enabling the determination of particle charge and momentum.

The primary challenge arises when dealing with multiple particles and the need to reconstruct their tracks. As the number of tracks increases, the time required for accurate track prediction also escalates. To address this challenge, we introduce the Line Segment Tracking (LST) algorithm, designed to reconstruct particle trajectories incrementally through linear segments. LST assembles these tracks piece by piece, allowing for massive parallelization of the process. Its objective is to identify all correct hits while discarding incorrect ones, enhancing efficiency. The intrinsic sequential nature of LST, however, imposes limitations in handling increasingly complex scenarios, highlighting the necessity of harnessing Machine Learning (ML) techniques.

Our architecture employs Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) with varying hidden layer sizes to process Linear Segments (LS), ultimately determining the track's authenticity. The DNN focuses on identifying line segments aligning with real particle trajectories. Furthermore, we conducted an extensive comparison between the DNN and a Graph Neural Network (GNN) for track classification, evaluating metrics such as Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves to assess performance.

While LST excels in parallelization, ML techniques like DNNs present promise in potentially improving track reconstruction accuracy. Despite the compute-intensive nature of ML, ongoing research in ML inference platforms aims to achieve high speeds, which could prove beneficial, particularly in the context of the High-Luminosity Large Hadron Collider (HL-LHC). Our results reveal that the DNN performs comparably to the simplest GNN, emphasizing the synergy between advanced ML and the progressive analysis of particle tracks."

Please review this revised summary and let me know if there are any further adjustments or specific points you'd like to highlight.

---

# you asked

message time: 2026-03-30 19:10:47

And let me give you the final info dup of other things to consider for your summary: In the realm of particle physics, the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) stands as a colossal accelerator in Geneva, Switzerland, with its intricate network of superconducting magnets propelling particles to immense energies for experimental collisions. Within the LHC, the Compact Muon Solenoid (CMS) experiment captures the paths of charged particles through a powerful magnetic field, aiming to distinguish accurate tracks amidst the complex particle interactions. Addressing the challenge of efficient track identification, the Line Segment Tracking (LST) algorithm emerges as a solution, reconstructing particle trajectories piece-by-piece, forming linear segments. Notably, LST's modular approach allows for parallelizability, a crucial attribute in tackling the intricate scenarios posed by the forthcoming High-Luminosity LHC (HL-LHC). While LST thrives in parallel processing, it faces limitations in handling increasingly complex scenarios sequentially, thereby highlighting the imperative of harnessing the power of Machine Learning (ML) techniques. This pivotal role of ML is exemplified in our architecture, which leverages Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) with varying hidden layer sizes to process Linear Segments (LS), culminating in an output neuron discerning the authenticity of the track. The convergence of the loss function during training, influenced by the hidden layer size and model hyperparameters, underscores the symbiotic relationship between advanced ML and the progressive analysis of particle tracks.

Results
As per the poster data, the training aspect of the model consisted in training two types of DNNs:

Small DNN: A DNN where its arquitechture consists of 2 hidden layers of 32 neurons each and was trained with a learning rate of 0.002, a batch size of 1000 data entries per batch and for 50 epochs.
Big DNN: A DNN wherre its arquitecture consists of 2 hidden layers of 200 neurons each and was trained with a learning rate of 0.002, a batch size of 1000 data entries per batch and for 100 epochs.
The focus of our results will be on those obtained with the aforementioned Big DNN. The loss curve plot of both the training and testing datasets indicates that the model is indeed learning patters in the training dataset that are applicable to those in the testing dataset and any other dataset for that matter; because both curves are trending downwards. If both curves where to diverge in any point in the epochs, we say taht the model is overfitting; simply put, that the model learned too much to the point that it became quite specialized in detecting patters of the training dataset only.

The prediction scores histogram indicates us at a glance that the vast mayority of data entries of our testing dataset are indeed labeled as Fake and that the model is predicting them as such, hence the distribution of Fake LS near the origin. On a similar fashion, the rest of the LS that are labeled as Real are distributed to the far right, indicating that the model indeed is predicting those Real LS as Real. Nevertheless, we can appreciate a little overlap of Real LS on top of the Fake LS of the far left of the plot, which means that the model mispredicted certain LS that are labeled as Real, and classified them as Fake; this a given with models related to Binary Classification.

On a related matter to the histogram overlaps, a better way to understand the rate at which is expected that the model will make this mispredictions iw with the help of a ROC Curve, which at a glance is telling us if the model is peforming accurate estimations of the Real LS (TPR ) in contrast to those Fake LS that are mispredicted as Real (FPR). For the context of the comparison of the GNN vs the DNN, this curve tell us exactly by how much the model's peformance is similar to one another. On the first ROC Curve, we can observe tht the peformance of the Small DNN is actually worse in comparison to the GNN, but in the case of the Big DNN versus the GNN, we observe that this DNN is doing almost the same work with a simpler arquitechture in contrast to the complex nature of the GNN, which for technical reasos such as the time of training and the time of developing the model pipeline, the Big DNN is better for our sake and purposes of classifying LS.

Continuing with the same plot, we plotted two square dots to further reference the estimated coordinates of where we could find a TPR > 0.95, and a TPR > 0.99 respectively, which in turn, can tell us the actual threshold to use for the model to actually comply with these TPR ranges. The followup tables contain the distilled numbers of the total of LS that both the DNN and GNN classified given their respective threshold that satisfy the previous TPR boundaries.

On both Table 1 and Table 2 (also included in the poster) we observe that we get a similar distribution (Predicted Scores Histogram) of data that was predicted as Real and Fake, the interesing detail is that, using the same testing datasete for both inferences with the DNN and the GNN, these models are using a substantial amount of the same LS for their predictions.

DNN > X	GNN > Y	Both
Real & Fake	134876	128475	106670
Real	49628	49628	48966
Fake	85248	78847	57704
Table 1. Table of LS selected for a TPR > 0.95. Note: X = 0.0328, Y = 0.0385.

DNN > X	GNN > Y	Both
Real & Fake	302497	313556	245207
Real	51716	51717	51446
Fake	250781	261839	193761
Table 2. Table of LS selected for a TPR > 0.99. Note: X = 0.0032, Y = 0.0044.

Thank you for providing this additional information. I'll integrate it into the existing text to create a comprehensive summary.

"In the realm of particle physics, the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) stands as a colossal accelerator in Geneva, Switzerland, with its intricate network of superconducting magnets propelling particles to immense energies for experimental collisions. The LHC, constructed in 2008, features a 27km ring of superconducting magnets equipped with accelerating structures that elevate particle energy levels. These structures, comprising around 1,600 magnets of diverse sizes and shapes, facilitate particle collisions at various points within the LHC, powering a multitude of experiments. Among these experiments, the Compact Muon Solenoid (CMS) is distinguished by an inner solenoid magnet generating a robust magnetic field that bends charged particles' trajectories, enabling the determination of particle charge and momentum.

The primary challenge arises when dealing with multiple particles and the need to reconstruct their tracks. As the number of tracks increases, the time required for accurate track prediction also escalates. To address this challenge, we introduce the Line Segment Tracking (LST) algorithm, designed to reconstruct particle trajectories piece-by-piece, forming linear segments. Notably, LST's modular approach allows for parallelizability, a crucial attribute in tackling the intricate scenarios posed by the forthcoming High-Luminosity LHC (HL-LHC). While LST thrives in parallel processing, it faces limitations in handling increasingly complex scenarios sequentially, thereby highlighting the imperative of harnessing the power of Machine Learning (ML) techniques.

Our architecture employs Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) with varying hidden layer sizes to process Linear Segments (LS), ultimately determining the track's authenticity. The DNN focuses on identifying line segments aligning with real particle trajectories.

**Results**

The focus of our results lies with the Big DNN, where the training consisted of two types of DNNs:

1. **Small DNN**: This DNN features 2 hidden layers of 32 neurons each and was trained with a learning rate of 0.002, a batch size of 1000 data entries per batch, and for 50 epochs.

2. **Big DNN**: In contrast, the Big DNN boasts 2 hidden layers of 200 neurons each and was trained with a learning rate of 0.002, a batch size of 1000 data entries per batch, and for 100 epochs.

The loss curve plot of both the training and testing datasets indicates that the model is indeed learning patterns in the training dataset applicable to those in the testing dataset. The convergence of both curves suggests that the model avoids overfitting and maintains its ability to generalize beyond the training data.

The prediction scores histogram reveals that the majority of data entries in our testing dataset are labeled as 'Fake,' aligning with the model's predictions. Similarly, 'Real' LS entries are primarily distributed to the far right of the plot, indicating accurate predictions. However, some overlap occurs, indicating misclassifications of 'Real' LS as 'Fake,' a common challenge in binary classification models.

To assess the model's performance, we employed Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves. The ROC curve for the Big DNN demonstrated comparable performance to a Graph Neural Network (GNN), showcasing the DNN's effectiveness despite its simpler architecture. We pinpointed thresholds corresponding to True Positive Rates (TPR) of 0.95 and 0.99 to understand misclassification rates.

Tables 1 and 2 detail the LS selected by both the DNN and GNN models for TPR > 0.95 and TPR > 0.99, respectively. These tables illustrate the models' usage of a substantial number of the same LS from the testing dataset for their predictions.

**Table 1.** LS selected for a TPR > 0.95. Note: DNN > 0.0328, GNN > 0.0385.

**Table 2.** LS selected for a TPR > 0.99. Note: DNN > 0.0032, GNN > 0.0044.

These results underscore the potential of advanced ML techniques, such as DNNs, to complement established methods in the analysis of particle tracks, promising improved accuracy and efficiency for future experiments at the HL-LHC."

# you asked

message time: 2026-03-30 19:10:47

Thank you so much! So then, the LST is the one that creates the data that the ML models classify as truthful or not? Please ellaborate based on what I provided you

---

# gemini response

Certainly, let me clarify the role of Line Segment Tracking (LST) and how it interacts with the machine learning (ML) models.

LST, which stands for Line Segment Tracking, is an algorithm designed to reconstruct particle trajectories within the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) experiment. It works by building these trajectories piece-by-piece, forming linear segments. This process involves analyzing and processing the data generated by the CMS (Compact Muon Solenoid) detector, which captures the paths of charged particles as they move through the powerful magnetic field inside the LHC.

Here's a step-by-step breakdown of how LST works and its relationship with ML models:

1. **Data Collection**: The CMS detector captures vast amounts of data representing the paths of charged particles within the LHC. This data is essentially a complex set of signals and measurements resulting from particle interactions.

2. **Line Segment Tracking**: LST takes this raw data and processes it, segmenting the particle trajectories into linear segments. These linear segments are the smallest components of a full particle track. LST essentially identifies and isolates these segments within the data.

3. **Parallel Processing**: LST's key strength lies in its ability to work in parallel. It doesn't rely on a sequential approach, which means it can process multiple segments concurrently. This feature is crucial for handling the large number of particle tracks in complex scenarios, especially in the High-Luminosity LHC (HL-LHC) environment.

4. **Segment Validation**: After identifying these linear segments, LST's goal is to determine whether each segment corresponds to a real particle trajectory or if it's a spurious or "fake" segment due to noise or other factors. This is where the machine learning models come into play.

5. **Machine Learning Classification**: LST generates features or characteristics of each linear segment that are relevant for determining its authenticity. These features could include information about the curvature, energy deposition, or other properties of the segment.

6. **ML Model Training**: The machine learning models, in this case, Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), are trained using labeled data. This data consists of linear segments that have been manually classified as either "real" or "fake." The DNNs learn to recognize patterns and features that distinguish between real and fake segments.

7. **Segment Classification**: Once trained, the DNNs can autonomously classify new linear segments produced by LST as either real or fake based on the features they've learned during training.

8. **Efficiency and Accuracy**: The classification provided by the ML models adds an extra layer of efficiency and accuracy to the LST process. Instead of relying solely on manual or rule-based methods to validate segments, the models can quickly and accurately assess the authenticity of each segment.

In summary, LST plays a fundamental role in processing the raw data generated by the CMS detector and segmenting it into linear segments representing particle trajectories. These segments are then validated using machine learning models, particularly DNNs, to determine whether they correspond to real particle tracks or are false detections. This combined approach leverages the strengths of both LST's parallel processing capabilities and ML's pattern recognition, enhancing the accuracy and efficiency of track identification in particle physics experiments at the LHC.
